g.data-narration: Welcome to understanding the system boot process. Learn how computers start from power-on to login screen.
System boot is loading the OS kernel into memory when power is turned on, called bootstrapping.
g.text: System boot (bootstrapping) is the process of loading the OS
kernel into memory when the computer is powered on.

g.data-narration: The boot sequence flows from power-on through BIOS, bootloader, kernel, init, to login.
BIOS is traditional firmware in ROM, while UEFI is modern with GUI, Secure Boot, and GPT support.
POST performs hardware diagnostics: CPU test, memory check, device detection before booting.
g.text: BIOS/UEFI runs POST to check hardware before booting:

g.data-narration: MBR uses 512-byte boot sector with 2TB limit. GPT has no limit with 128 partitions and backups.
GRUB bootloader has three stages: Stage 1 loads Stage 1.5 with drivers, then Stage 2 shows boot menu.
Kernel decompresses, initializes CPU/memory, mounts root filesystem using drivers from initrd.
g.text: initrd/initramfs: Temporary filesystem with drivers to access real root

g.data-narration: Init process (PID 1) starts all system services. Modern systems use systemd with targets and units.
g.text: Init Process (PID 1) - Parent of All Processes
Targets: multi-user.target, graphical.target | Units: service, socket
Example: systemctl start sshd | systemctl enable nginx

g.data-narration: Boot can originate from hard disk, USB, CD, or network via PXE for diskless workstations.
PXE boot uses DHCP for IP address and TFTP to download boot image from network server.
Complete sequence: Power On → BIOS/UEFI → POST → Bootloader → Kernel → init → Login Screen.
Key exam points: Bootstrap definition, boot sequence, BIOS vs UEFI differences, MBR vs GPT limits, GRUB stages.
g.text: Bootstrap program = first code in firmware | Boot: BIOS→POST→GRUB→Kernel→Init→Login
MBR: 2TB/4 partitions | GPT: No limit/128 partitions
